chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly states in the Abstract and Introduction that source code, container images, and a dataset of 6400 intrusion traces are available. The References section (Ref [27] and [28]) provides specific URLs to Zenodo for the supplementary material/code and GitHub for the CSLE traces.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation is conducted in an emulation environment using real hardware (a cluster of 13 physical servers with Docker containers) connected via Ethernet, rather than a pure software simulation like ns-3. This constitutes a physical testbed/emulation hybrid approach.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: Section II ('THE INTRUSION TOLERANCE USE CASE') and Section IV contain a formal definition of the threat model. It explicitly defines the attacker's capabilities (Byzantine behavior, no physical access, cannot forge signatures) and assumptions (hybrid failure model), satisfying the requirement for a clear, explicit section.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides rigorous trade-off analysis in Section V and VIII. It quantifies costs via an objective function J_i involving recovery frequency and time-to-recovery, analyzes computational overhead of algorithms (Table 2), and evaluates performance metrics like availability vs. operational cost (Fig. 11, Table 7).
chain_of_thought_evaluation.major_weakness: While the paper scores high on all criteria, the total score is capped at 8 because Q1 (Reproducibility) received a perfect score but relies on external supplementary material links rather than embedding direct working URLs in the main text body for immediate access.
